region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Mutton Bhuna; mapped to the closest parent class 'mutton dish' (FOODON_03315595). Exact bhuna preparation not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (sheep meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. No specific bhuna or dry-fried mutton curry code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Mutton Bhuna or bhuna-style mutton curry identified in the SNOMED CT international release as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mutton Bhuna in Singapore is approximately 200g, representing a moderate portion of the thick, spiced mutton curry served as a main dish alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Bhuna — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Bhuna (cr_id G00010659), confidence 0.95. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB No. 17290) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate meat-based curry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Mutton Bhuna exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous meat-based curry dishes with low carbohydrate content (7.44g/100g). The dominant carbohydrate source is onion and tomato, both low-GI vegetables. Glycemic load per 200g serving calculated as: (30 × 7.44 × 2) / 100 = 4.5g. This is a conservative analogous estimate consistent with published GI values for similar South Asian meat curries.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold; potassium at 210mg/100g is marginally above the 200mg rescue floor but dietary fibre at 0.3g/100g is far below the 3g/100g rescue threshold, preventing borderline rescue.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Mutton Bhuna to small portions (≤100g) and request reduced-salt preparation; pairing with high-potassium vegetables such as spinach or brinjal can partially offset the sodium load, but the dish as standardly prepared is not suitable for unrestricted consumption in hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.44g/100g) and high protein (14.05g/100g) from mutton slow gastric emptying and blunt post-prandial glucose response; estimated GI of 30 places this dish in the low-GI category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mutton Bhuna is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (150–200g) without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; the protein-dominant macronutrient profile supports glycaemic control, but caloric density (192 kcal/100g) warrants portion awareness.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.8g/100g from mutton, cholesterol at 85mg/100g, and sodium at 467mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk; trans fat at 0.1g/100g is additionally pro-atherogenic.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Mutton Bhuna infrequently and in small portions (≤100g); substituting mutton with lean chicken or fish, reducing cooking oil, and lowering added salt would substantially improve the cardiovascular risk profile of this dish.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6; trans fat at 0.1g/100g is additionally pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Mutton Bhuna; the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) present in the dish are insufficient to counteract the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from red meat, and substitution with chicken or oily fish is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Bhuna (also known as Bhuna Gosht) is a rich, intensely flavoured South Asian dry-style mutton curry prepared by the bhuna technique — a method of cooking meat in its own juices over high heat with aromatic spices until the sauce is reduced to a thick, clinging coating. The dish originates from the Indian subcontinent and is widely consumed in Singapore's Indian-Muslim and South Indian communities, commonly served at hawker centres and Indian restaurants as a lunch or dinner main course alongside steamed white rice, roti prata, or naan. The primary ingredient is mutton (goat or lamb), slow-cooked with onion, tomato, ginger, garlic, turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, chilli, and garam masala. The resulting dish is dark, aromatic, and moderately spiced, with a tender meat texture and a thick, almost dry gravy. Nutritionally, Mutton Bhuna provides 192 kcal per 100g, with a protein-dominant macronutrient profile (14.05g protein, 11.58g fat, 7.44g carbohydrate). Sodium is elevated at 467mg/100g, making the dish a concern for hypertensive individuals. The low carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in moderate portions. Saturated fat and cholesterol levels warrant caution for cardiovascular disease patients. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.

How many calories in mutton bhuna?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Mutton Bhuna (cr_id G00010659) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb/mutton data. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence sub-field) documented with full basis. Overall record confidence is high given the primary HPB source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8g/100g) is an adapted estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked lamb/mutton (NDB No. 17290), as the HPB Foodo record does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is documented as an adapted value in nutrient_source. 2. Trans fat (0.1g/100g) is carried forward from the input health_suitability caution note; this value is not explicitly reported in the HPB per_100g block and should be verified against the original HPB source record. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, water content, and full vitamin/mineral panel for this traditional mutton bhuna preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for G00010659 as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked lamb) was consulted but values were not mapped due to preparation-method differences. 4. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mutton Bhuna exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16. 5. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315595 and FoodEx2 code A0FJP are approximate matches; no exact ontology code for bhuna-style mutton curry exists in either system as of 2024. 6. Halal status is assumed based on traditional preparation without pork or alcohol; individual restaurant preparation should be verified.
